title: Quiz: Exotic Options
description: Test your understanding of exotic options including barrier, Asian, lookback, and digital options

questions.question: What happens to a down-and-out call option when the underlying price falls below the barrier?

options.text: The option becomes more valuable
The option is knocked out and becomes worthless
The option is activated and starts paying off
The strike price is adjusted to the barrier level

questions.explanation: A down-and-out option is a knock-out option that becomes worthless (expires with zero value) when the underlying price hits or falls below the barrier level.

questions.question: Why are Asian options typically cheaper than vanilla options with the same strike?

options.text: They have shorter maturities
Averaging reduces volatility of the payoff
They have higher barriers
They pay in a different currency

questions.explanation: Asian options use an average price which has lower volatility than the spot price (averages smooth out fluctuations). This reduced volatility leads to lower option premiums.

questions.question: What is the payoff of a floating-strike lookback call at expiration?

options.text: S_T - K (final price minus strike)
S_max - K (maximum price minus strike)
S_T - S_min (final price minus minimum price)
S_avg - K (average price minus strike)

questions.explanation: A floating-strike lookback call effectively lets you buy at the lowest price during the option's life. The payoff is S_T - S_min (final price minus the minimum observed price).

questions.question: Why don't arithmetic Asian options have closed-form pricing solutions?

options.text: They are too simple to need complex formulas
The sum of lognormal variables is not lognormal
They were invented too recently
The Black-Scholes model doesn't apply to them

questions.explanation: While the geometric mean of lognormal variables is lognormal (enabling closed-form solutions), the arithmetic mean of lognormals does not have a known distribution, requiring numerical methods like Monte Carlo.

questions.question: A digital (binary) call option pays a fixed amount if the underlying finishes above the strike, regardless of how far above.

options.text: True
False

questions.explanation: True. Digital options have discontinuous payoffs. A digital call pays a fixed amount (e.g., $1) if S_T > K, and zero otherwise. The magnitude by which S_T exceeds K doesn't affect the payout.

questions.question: How are lookback options priced compared to vanilla options?

options.text: Much cheaper because they are riskier
About the same price
Much more expensive because they guarantee optimal entry/exit
Their price cannot be determined

questions.explanation: Lookback options are significantly more expensive than vanilla options because they eliminate the risk of suboptimal timing. The holder can effectively buy at the minimum or sell at the maximum price over the option's life.

questions.question: Which exotic option type is particularly useful for hedging commodity prices over an averaging period?

options.text: Barrier options
Digital options
Asian options
Compound options

questions.explanation: Asian options are ideal for hedging exposures based on average prices, such as commodity purchases made over time. Airlines hedging jet fuel or manufacturers hedging raw materials often use Asian options.

questions.question: Which are characteristics of path-dependent exotic options? (Select all that apply)

options.text: Payoff depends on the entire price history
Generally require Monte Carlo simulation for pricing
Are always more expensive than vanilla options
Are good candidates for quantum computing speedups

questions.explanation: Path-dependent options depend on price history (a), typically require Monte Carlo (b), and are good quantum computing candidates (d). However, they are NOT always more expensive - barrier options are cheaper than vanilla due to the knock-out risk.
